title: Cooking Oil on Leather
timeEstimate: Pro-lane for any meaningful spot; home is blot + condition only
stainChemistry: Cooking oil is long-chain triglycerides — thin, penetrates fast. On leather, oil soaks through any wear spots in the topcoat into the dyed hide and natural fiber. Oxidized cooking oil leaves a darker shadow that's nearly impossible to lift without re-dyeing. Critical leather rules: NO household solvents (acetone, undiluted alcohol, mineral spirits) — strip finish on contact. NO oxidizers (peroxide, OxiClean) — strip finish + dye + oils. The cornstarch-preload trick that works on cotton/carpet partially helps on leather (powder absorbs surface oil) but won't reach oil that's penetrated past the topcoat. Pro uses controlled VDS (volatile dry solvent) at strict dilution + immediate re-conditioning.
whyThisWorks: Cornstarch / fuller's earth preload pulls surface oil up via capillary action without water or solvent risk. Mild soap on barely-damp cloth handles surface residue. Pro VDS for residual penetrated oil. Conditioner restores hide. Home protocol: powder + mild wipe + condition; pro for anything more.
defaultAssumption: Finished leather with topcoat. Aniline = pro from minute one (oil soaks straight in). Most car interior / handbag / shoe leather is finished; most leather furniture too. Suede: different card entirely.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Drop of water, cornstarch / fuller's earth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Water-drop test: beads = finished, soaks = aniline (pro from minute one). Cover oil with cornstarch or fuller's earth. 30–60 min absorb. Brush off softly. Repeat with fresh powder if first layer saturated.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 1–2 drops Dawn in warm water on white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dawn in warm water, wring nearly dry. Wipe outside-in. Plain-water-damp wipe to clear soap. Leather should never look wet.

spottingProtocol.equipment: VDS per pro reference + leather conditioner
spottingProtocol.instruction: Pro applies VDS to a white cloth, controlled tamp on residual penetrated oil. Strict dilution + immediate conditioning. Home cannot replicate.
Mandatory after any wet or solvent treatment. Cleaning + solvent strips natural oils.

steps: 1. Water-drop leather test. Aniline = pro from minute one.
2. Cornstarch / fuller's earth preload 30–60 min. Brush off.
3. Mild soap on barely-damp cloth, outside-in.
4. Plain-water damp wipe to clear soap.
professionalProtocol.temperature: ambient — no heat (heat dries leather + polymerizes oil)
warnings: NEVER hydrogen peroxide / OxiClean / chlorine — strip leather permanently
NEVER acetone / undiluted alcohol / mineral spirits at home — destroy leather finish
Aniline = pro from minute one (oil soaks directly into dyed hide)

homeSolutions.agent: Identify Your Leather First — One Drop of Water
homeSolutions.instruction: Water-drop test on hidden leather: beads = finished (proceed cautiously). Soaks in = aniline (STOP, pro from minute one). Aniline absorbs oil directly into the dyed hide; home cleaning won't reach penetrated oil. Don't gamble.

The internet's worst leather + oil advice: hairspray (alcohol strips leather), acetone, mineral spirits, peroxide, OxiClean, chlorine bleach. ALL strip the finish + dye + oils permanently. Damage worse than the cooking oil. The cornstarch + soap path below is the only safe home approach.

Cover the oil spot with cornstarch (or baking soda, or fuller's earth). Let sit 30–60 min. The powder wicks surface oil UP via capillary action — no water, no solvent, no risk. Brush off softly with a soft brush. If the powder visibly saturated, repeat with fresh layer. Most surface oil comes off this way.

homeSolutions.agent: Mild Soap on a BARELY-Damp Cloth
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix 1 to 2 drops of Dawn (just two drops) in a cup of warm water. Dampen a clean white cloth, WRING IT OUT until barely damp. Wipe the area in gentle circles, OUTSIDE-IN. Then wipe with a plain-water-damp cloth to clear soap. Leather should never look visibly wet.

Apply leather conditioner (Lexol or Bickmore) per the label. Cleaning + cooking oil both strip leather's natural oils — conditioning prevents cracking AND fills micro-gaps that residual oil could pool in.

homeSolutions.agent: Pro for Anything Remaining — What to Tell Them
homeSolutions.instruction: Take to a leather care specialist (NOT a regular dry cleaner). Tell them: "Cooking oil on [finished / aniline / unknown] leather, age [hours / days], one home pass with cornstarch preload + mild soap + barely-damp cloth + conditioner. No oxidizer, no acetone, no alcohol, no mineral spirits used." Pros use VDS at controlled dilution + re-condition — chemistry home cannot do safely.

steps: 1. Water-drop test. Aniline = pro from minute one.
2. NO oxidizer, NO acetone, NO alcohol, NO hairspray, NO mineral spirits.
4. Mild soap (1–2 drops Dawn) on a BARELY-damp cloth, outside-in.
5. Plain-water damp wipe to clear soap.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if leather is aniline, if oil remains after powder + mild soap, or if anyone has applied harsh chemistry previously.
warnings: NEVER undiluted alcohol or mineral spirits
NEVER hydrogen peroxide / OxiClean / chlorine bleach

neverDo: Never acetone / undiluted alcohol / mineral spirits on leather
fiberSensitivities: Finished / pigmented leather: tolerates cornstarch + mild soap + conditioner
Suede: own protocol (no liquid, no powder paste)

materialWarnings: Cornstarch + mild soap + condition is the safe home path
Conditioner after every wet treatment, mandatory

escalation.when: Aniline / unfinished leather (always), oil remaining after powder + mild soap pass, or anyone has applied harsh chemistry previously.
escalation.whatToTell: "Cooking oil on [finished / aniline / unknown] leather, age [hours / days], one home pass with cornstarch preload + mild soap + barely-damp cloth + conditioner. No oxidizer, no acetone, no alcohol, no mineral spirits used."
escalation.specialistType: leather care specialist (NOT a regular dry cleaner)

contextQuestions: Water-drop test: bead, soak, or in-between?
Type of cooking oil — olive, canola, peanut?
Has anyone applied any solvent or oxidizer?
How long ago — fresh or aged (oxidized)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Aniline leather absorbs oil directly — pro from minute one. Aged oxidized oil leaves a darker shadow that's nearly permanent — set expectations honestly. Light leather shows residual more.
